titleStmt.title: Letter from Amelia Jephson to Nancy O'Rahilly, 14 June 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Amelia Jephson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Amelia Jephson to Nancy O'Rahilly (1875-1961) on the 14th of June 1916. The writer apologises for the long lapse of time before writing and asks for a memento of her 'wonderful & heroic husband'. She did not hear of the memorial Mass in time - otherwise she would have been there. She proposes that she and Nancy do Lough Derg together. Finally, the writer indicates that she could visit Nancy. in conjunction to her regular visits to Lansdowne Road to see her ailing motherNancy O'Rahilly was a member of the Provisional Council of Cumann na mBan at its founding in 1914. She was the wife of Michael Joseph O'Rahilly, self-described as 'The' O'Rahilly (1875-1916), who was shot in Parnell/Moore Street on 28 April 1916 and died some time later. Nancy was pregnant at the time of her husband's death – the child was born three months later.

correspAction.placeName: 45 Albert Road, Glenageary, Co. Dublin, Ireland.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
45 Albert Road Glenageary 14th June/16 Dear Mrs. O'Rahilly It is only now - after this
long lapse of time - that I
can bring myself to try and
tell you how I have felt and
prayed for you ever since
I knew that a mere letter
of sympathy could only be
poor comfort in such trouble
as yours. To say nothing
of your irreparable loss
the anxieties of Easter
Week must have been
appalling, but please God
you will soon have a great â
if not the greatest â consolation. Perhaps some time, later
on, you could find me some
small memento â a prayer book
or any little belonging of
your wonderful & heroic
husband â that is if you
can spare it to one with
so small a claim as I have. I wish I had known of
the Requiem at Donnybrook.
I only heard of it yesterday
or I should have been present,
but indeed I suppose it is
he who is praying for us
in poor distracted Ireland. I am waiting a few years
for L. Derg, so we may do it
together again. I suppose you would not
care to see me (?) but I am
very often in your neighbourhood
I go up constantly to
Lansdowne Road as my mother
has been laid up for the past
2 months with phlebitis.
Thank God she is now getting
better and will soon I hope
be able to get away for a
change. With renewed & deepest
sympathy
I remain dear Mrs O'Rahilly
Yrs ever most sincerely Amelia Jephson
